witnesses.description: A three-point sample lies exactly on y = 1 + 2x.
A non-perfect three-point sample has an exact least-squares projection.
The least-squares projection improves over the mean-only baseline.

checks.claim: The listed coefficients exactly fit the perfect-line dataset and satisfy the normal equations.
checks.notes: The validator recomputes X^T X, X^T y, fitted values, residuals, RSS, and the normal equations.

checks.claim: The listed coefficients are an exact least-squares projection for the non-perfect sample.
checks.notes: The validator checks the normal equations and the residual orthogonality condition X^T r = 0.

checks.claim: The listed least-squares fit improves residual sum of squares over the mean-only baseline.
checks.notes: The validator recomputes the baseline mean, residuals, RSS, and model improvement exactly.

checks.claim: The coefficients (1, 1) solve the non-perfect least-squares problem.
checks.notes: The validator rejects the claim because X^T y - X^T X beta is nonzero; the Axeyum regression refutes beta0 = 1, beta1 = 1, and the first normal equation 3*beta0 + 3*beta1 = 7 with rechecked Farkas evidence.

checks.claim: General regression statistics require future proof and numerical-honesty resources.

data.target_theorem_shape: Gauss-Markov optimality, inference for linear models, asymptotics, and model-selection guarantees
data.future_checker: Lean module plus numerical-honesty metadata for floating-point/statistical procedures

checks.notes: The finite rows are checked by exact rational replay; general regression theory remains a proof-assistant or numerical-honesty horizon.
